P001_S0001.quote: standard deviations below 1.0\% indicate that all performance differences are statistically significant
P001_S0001.reason: The perturbed text adds a false interpretive claim that small standard deviations imply statistical significance, which is a textbook misinterpretation — standard deviation magnitude alone does not establish statistical significance without a formal test.

P002_S0002.quote: significantly better than the DeepSeek GAT hybrid (98.25\%) because the standard deviations (0.62\% and 0.72\% respectively) do not overlap with the mean values
P002_S0002.reason: The surrounding_context shows the original text compared the best hybrid to GNN-only (90.56%) and frozen PLM (89.94%) baselines, not to another hybrid; the perturbed version misinterprets overlapping standard deviations as non-overlapping and draws an invalid significance conclusion.

P003_S0003.quote: achieving a better precision--recall trade-off is what allows a model to be smaller while maintaining performance
P003_S0003.reason: The surrounding_context states 'Hybrids based on the smaller Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B extractor achieve the highest AUPRC...indicating a better precision--recall trade-off despite the smaller backbone' — the original says the small size is the cause/context, not that better PR trade-off enables smaller size; the causal direction is reversed.
P003_S0003.original: On Devign (Table~\ref{tab:devign_results}), the picture is more nuanced. GNN-only models remain worse, but hybrids and frozen PLMs achieve similar macro-F1 after tuning the decision threshold on the validation set. Notably, AUPRC shows clearer differences: hybrids typically improve over their frozen PLM counterparts, except for DeepSeek where the frozen and GAT-based hybrid models overlap within standard deviation. Hybrids based on the smaller Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B extractor achieve the highest AUPRC (up to 73.80\% $\pm$ 0.56\%), indicating a better precision--recall trade-off despite the smaller backbone. Finally, since hybrid hyperparameters are transferred from Java250 whereas frozen PLM MLPs are tuned per task, the Devign comparison is conservative for hybrids.
P003_S0003.perturbed: On Devign (Table~\ref{tab:devign_results}), the picture is more nuanced. GNN-only models remain worse, but hybrids and frozen PLMs achieve similar macro-F1 after tuning the decision threshold on the validation set. Notably, AUPRC shows clearer differences: hybrids typically improve over their frozen PLM counterparts, except for DeepSeek where the frozen and GAT-based hybrid models overlap within standard deviation. The high AUPRC achieved by Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B (up to 73.80\% $\pm$ 0.56\%) suggests that achieving a better precision--recall trade-off is what allows a model to be smaller while maintaining performance.

P004_S0004.original:
%Overall, these results suggest that PLM$\rightarrow$GNN hybrids consistently dominate GNN-only models and improve ranking quality over frozen PLMs under class imbalance, while achieving similar F1 at the tuned operating point.
Overall, hybrids outperform GNN-only models and often improve AUPRC over frozen PLM baselines, while achieving comparable F1 at the validation-tuned operating point.

P006_S0006.quote: To investigate if high inference latency necessitates the use of hybrid architectures
P006_S0006.reason: The surrounding_context shows the original RQ2 section is about quantifying runtime cost of hybrids, not about whether latency necessitates hybrid use; the perturbed framing reverses the causal logic by implying latency drives the architectural choice.

P007_S0007.quote: a selected subset of the Devign test split (100 samples) where the GNN overhead was minimal

P007_S0007.original: To quantify the runtime cost of our hybrids, we separate (i) preprocessing (Table~\ref{tab:preprocessing_times}) from (ii) GNN inference (Table~\ref{tab:devign_inference_time}), and report wall-clock times on the Devign test split (2732 samples, batch size $=64$).

P008_S0008.quote: model parameter count has no correlation with inference speed
P008_S0008.reason: The surrounding_context states 'The choice of PLM extractor has a modest effect (Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B is consistently the fastest, StarCoder2-3B the slowest)', which shows a correlation between model size and speed, directly contradicting the perturbed conclusion.
P008_S0008.original: Table~\ref{tab:devign_inference_time} reports GNN inference times across all PLM$\rightarrow$GNN combinations. For a full pass over the test set, all hybrids run in roughly $10$--$12$ seconds, and differences between GNN architectures are small: for a fixed PLM, GAT, GCN, and GraphTransformer differ by well under two seconds. The choice of PLM extractor has a modest effect (Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B is consistently the fastest, StarCoder2-3B the slowest), but overall the incremental cost of the GNN layers on top of cached PLM features is low. The times shown in Table~\ref{tab:devign_inference_time} assume cached PLM and positional embeddings; therefore, they report the total time spent on GNN inference in all PLM$\rightarrow$GNN combinations.
P008_S0008.perturbed: Table~\ref{tab:devign_inference_time} reports GNN inference times across all PLM$\rightarrow$GNN combinations. For a full pass over the test set, all hybrids run in roughly $10$--$12$ seconds. Since the difference between the fastest (Qwen) and slowest (StarCoder) is only 2 seconds, we conclude that model parameter count has no correlation with inference speed.

P009_S0009.quote: the high cost of spectral positional encoding is driven by the bottleneck in the GNN inference pipeline
P009_S0009.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'the main bottleneck in the hybrid pipeline is not the GNN itself but the spectral positional encoding step' — the perturbed version reverses this causal direction, claiming GNN inference drives the positional encoding cost.
P009_S0009.original: Table~\ref{tab:preprocessing_times} breaks down the preprocessing pipeline. AST construction is relatively cheap ($\approx 23$ seconds for the full test set), whereas Laplacian positional encodings dominate the structural overhead ($\approx 255$ seconds), exceeding even the cost of PLM feature extraction for DeepSeek (162 s) and StarCoder (220 s), and remaining comparable to Qwen2.5-Coder (107 s). In other words, in our current implementation, the main bottleneck in the hybrid pipeline is not the GNN itself but the spectral positional encoding step.
P009_S0009.perturbed: In other words, in our current implementation, the high cost of spectral positional encoding is driven by the bottleneck in the GNN inference pipeline.

P000_S0010.quote: larger models such as Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B not only deliver strong AUPRC...but also reduce preprocessing and inference time compared to smaller extractors
P000_S0010.reason: The surrounding_context calls Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B a 'smaller' model and says it reduces time 'compared to larger extractors'; the perturbed version contradictorily labels it 'larger' and says it reduces time compared to 'smaller extractors'.
P000_S0010.original: These measurements suggest two practical takeaways. First, the structural side of the pipeline can be made substantially cheaper by replacing Laplacian eigenvector embeddings with lighter positional schemes, without changing the overall hybrid architecture. Second, the choice of PLM affects both accuracy and cost: smaller models such as Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B not only deliver strong AUPRC on Devign (Tables~\ref{tab:devign_results} and~\ref{tab:devign_obf_results}) but also reduce preprocessing and inference time compared to larger extractors. For this kind of hybrid design, compact PLMs therefore offer a more attractive performance–cost trade-off than larger backbones.
P000_S0010.perturbed: These measurements suggest two practical takeaways. First, the structural side of the pipeline can be made substantially cheaper by replacing Laplacian eigenvector embeddings with lighter positional schemes, without changing the overall hybrid architecture. Second, the choice of PLM affects both accuracy and cost: larger models such as Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B not only deliver strong AUPRC on Devign (Tables~\ref{tab:devign_results} and~\ref{tab:devign_obf_results}) but also reduce preprocessing and inference time compared to smaller extractors. For this kind of hybrid design, compact PLMs therefore offer a more attractive performance--cost trade-off than larger backbones.

                  & GAT              & 62.83\% (0.53\%)  & 63.09\% (0.58\%)  & 51.82\% (0.24\%)  & 82.47\% (2.41\%) \\

                  & Finetuned        & 63.73\%   & 65.20\%  & 50.06\%  & 93.47\% \\
      \addlinespace
      \multirow{5}{*}{\shortstack{StarCoder\\(\texttt{StarCoder2-3B})}}
                  & GAT              & 65.58\% (1.57\%)  & 63.58\% (1.03\%)  & 51.50\% (0.92\%)  & 85.57\% (5.56\%) \\
                  & GCN              & 63.51\% (0.46\%)  & 62.57\% (0.32\%)  & 50.78\% (1.00\%)  & 83.52\% (2.26\%) \\
                  & GraphTransformer & 64.86\% (1.03\%)  & 62.92\% (1.00\%)  & 52.02\% (1.07\%)  & 82.00\% (5.70\%) \\
                  & Frozen           & 63.45\% (0.69\%)  & 63.59\% (1.73\%)  & 51.45\% (1.26\%)  & 84.79\% (8.87\%) \\
                  & Finetuned        & 61.51\%  & 68.68\%  & 55.27\%  & 95.78\% \\
      \addlinespace
      \multirow{5}{*}{\shortstack{QwenCoder\\(\texttt{Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B})}}
                  & GAT              & 69.90\% (0.65\%)  & 63.45\% (0.49\%)  & 52.47\% (0.74\%)  & 82.44\% (2.97\%) \\
                  & GCN              & 69.80\% (0.40\%)  & 63.68\% (0.45\%)  & 50.54\% (2.02\%)  & 89.18\% (7.34\%) \\

                  & Frozen           & 63.77\% (0.50\%)  & 62.11\% (1.63\%)  & 52.24\% (1.18\%)  & 77.68\% (7.24\%) \\

  \caption{

P003_S0013.quote: the choice of threshold tuning makes the models significantly more robust than the architectural design
P003_S0013.reason: The surrounding_context states 'no family is dramatically more robust in terms of the operating point chosen by threshold tuning', meaning threshold tuning does not differentiate robustness; the perturbed version inverts this to claim threshold tuning is what makes models robust.
P003_S0013.original: Under identifier obfuscation on Devign (Table~\ref{tab:devign_obf_results}), all model families suffer a performance drop, but the degradation in F1 is relatively uniform across hybrids and PLM-only baselines. This indicates that no family is dramatically more robust in terms of the operating point chosen by threshold tuning.
P003_S0013.perturbed: Under identifier obfuscation on Devign (Table~\ref{tab:devign_obf_results}), all model families suffer a performance drop, but the degradation in F1 is relatively uniform across hybrids and PLM-only baselines. This indicates that the choice of threshold tuning makes the models significantly more robust than the architectural design.

P004_S0014.quote: Hybrids based on Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B exhibit the largest loss in AUPRC
P004_S0014.reason: The surrounding_context states 'Hybrids based on Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B exhibit the smallest loss in AUPRC compared to their non-obfuscated counterparts'; the perturbed version changes 'smallest' to 'largest', directly contradicting the original claim.
P004_S0014.original: In contrast, AUPRC reveals clearer differences. Hybrids based on Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B exhibit the smallest loss in AUPRC compared to their non-obfuscated counterparts, suggesting slightly better robustness in terms of precision--recall trade-off. Moreover, for both StarCoder and QwenCoder, the hybrid PLM$\rightarrow$GNN models lose less AUPRC than the corresponding frozen PLM baselines, indicating that injecting structural information helps preserve ranking quality under identifier perturbations.
P004_S0014.perturbed: In contrast, AUPRC reveals clearer differences. Hybrids based on Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B exhibit the largest loss in AUPRC compared to their non-obfuscated counterparts, suggesting slightly better robustness in terms of precision--recall trade-off. Moreover, for both StarCoder and QwenCoder, the hybrid PLM$\rightarrow$GNN models lose less AUPRC than the corresponding frozen PLM baselines, indicating that injecting structural information helps preserve ranking quality under identifier perturbations.

P005_S0015.quote: the choice of GNN matters more for robustness than the choice of PLM, despite the data showing larger variance when changing the PLM
P005_S0015.reason: The surrounding_context states 'the choice of PLM matters more for robustness than the choice of GNN architecture'; the perturbed version reverses this causal attribution while self-contradictorily acknowledging the data shows larger variance when changing the PLM.
P005_S0015.original: These results suggest that robustness is primarily inherited from the semantic feature extractor: within a given PLM, the three GNN variants achieve very similar AUPRC values whose differences fall within one standard deviation, whereas changing the PLM has a larger effect. In other words, the choice of PLM matters more for robustness than the choice of GNN architecture, but GNN hybrids still provide a consistent advantage over using the PLM alone.
P005_S0015.perturbed: These results suggest that the choice of GNN architecture primarily determines the robustness of the semantic feature extractor: within a given PLM, the three GNN variants achieve very similar AUPRC values whose differences fall within one standard deviation, whereas changing the PLM has a larger effect. In other words, the choice of GNN matters more for robustness than the choice of PLM, despite the data showing larger variance when changing the PLM.

P006_S0016.quote: GNN-only models as shown in Table~\ref{tab:devign_results} remain the strongest across all settings
P006_S0016.reason: The surrounding_context states 'GNN-only models remain the weakest across all settings'; the perturbed version changes 'weakest' to 'strongest', directly contradicting the established finding.
P006_S0016.original: Finally, GNN-only models as shown in Table~\ref{tab:devign_results} remain the weakest across all settings, confirming that structural information by itself cannot compensate for the loss of semantic cues from identifiers and surrounding context.
P006_S0016.perturbed: Finally, GNN-only models as shown in Table~\ref{tab:devign_results} remain the strongest across all settings, confirming that structural information by itself cannot compensate for the loss of semantic cues from identifiers and surrounding context.

P009_S0019.reason: The surrounding_context shows the original comment is simply '% ANOVA : Java250' with no exclusions; the perturbed version adds 'Excluding non-significant GNN variants', introducing p-hacking by selectively excluding variants from the analysis.
